title: Project Freedom: 1,500 Ships Stuck, 2 Cleared, One 'Dome'
narrative: The Pentagon and Trump administration claim "Project Freedom" has cleared a safe shipping lane through the Strait of Hormuz. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth called the US military presence a "red, white and blue dome" protecting commerce. Joint Chiefs Chairman Gen. Dan Caine said the lane is clear and attacks "do not meet the threshold" of violating the ceasefire. The public message: the US has opened the waterway and commerce can resume.
telemetry: Only 2 US-flag ships crossed the Strait on the first day of the so-called "cleared lane"
~1,500 ships remain stuck in the Persian Gulf, including ~1,000 large ocean-going vessels
Iran publicly states it still controls the strait and non-approved routes will face "decisive response"
Pentagon's own framing: attacks don't meet the ceasefire-violation threshold — redefining ongoing attacks as non-violations
European allies (UK, France) refuse to participate until a "permanent ceasefire" — an open-ended condition Iran controls
Brent crude at $109/barrel, up 44% since the war began; WTI at $105
Oil price gains happened the same week Trump's rhetoric "dented hopes" of a Hormuz deal
analysis: The administration says the lane is open. The evidence says otherwise. Two ships crossed on day one of Project Freedom. Approximately 1,500 remain trapped in the Persian Gulf. That's a ratio of 750 to 1 between ships waiting and ships cleared. Hegseth's "red, white and blue dome" sounds like a defense of the homeland — but what it describes is a security theater with a maritime traffic problem. The ceasefire redefinition is the most technically revealing detail: by redefining ongoing attacks as non-violations of a ceasefire, the Pentagon avoids having to acknowledge that the strait is still being contested. You don't get to say a lane is "clear" when 1,500 vessels are waiting for conditions that Iran controls to improve. The Europeans aren't waiting. They want a permanent ceasefire — which Iran controls the conditions for. Brent crude at $109 tells you what the market thinks about "Project Freedom."
verdict: [SIPHONED VERDICT]: Project Freedom has cleared a lane in name only. Two ships crossed; 1,500 did not. The gap between the operational claim and the physical reality is not a communication failure — it's the gap between a political objective and a fact on the water. Hegseth's dome doesn't protect a shipping corridor when ships aren't using it. The ceasefire redefinition — attacks that don't meet the threshold — is a euphemism for ongoing intimidation that the administration has decided not to classify as a problem. Brent crude at $109 and 1,500 ships waiting is what a functioning blockade looks like. Calling it a cleared lane doesn't change it.
